blocks:
Ayahs

إِذَا ٱلسَّمَآءُ ٱنفَطَرَتْ (١)
When the sky is rent apart, (1)
وَإِذَا ٱلْكَوَاكِبُ ٱنتَثَرَتْ (٢)
when the stars are scattered, (2)
وَإِذَا ٱلْبِحَارُ فُجِّرَتْ (٣)
when the seas are merged, (3)
وَإِذَا ٱلْقُبُورُ بُعْثِرَتْ (٤)
when the graves are overturned, (4)
عَلِمَتْ نَفْسٌ مَّا قَدَّمَتْ وَأَخَّرَتْ (٥)
then a soul shall know what it has sent ahead and left behind. (5)

Content of Surah Al-Infitar

This Surah, like many other Surahs in the final part of the Quran, revolves around matters related to the Resurrection. Overall, its nineteen verses point to five topics in this regard:

  1. Ashrat al-Sa'ah: Meaning the momentous events that occur at the end of the world and on the threshold of the Resurrection.

  2. Directing man's attention to the blessings of Allah which encompass his entire being, and breaking his arrogance so that he may prepare himself for the Hereafter.

  3. Reference to the angels who are commissioned to record the deeds of human beings.

  4. The destiny of the righteous and the wicked on the Day of Judgment.

  5. A glimpse into the hardships of that Great Day.

Virtue of Reciting this Surah

In a hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام, we read:

من قرأ هاتين السورتين "إِذَا السَّماءُ انْفَطَرَتْ" وإِذَا السَّماءُ انْشَقَّتْ و جعلهما نصب عينه فى صلاة الفريضة و النافلة لم يحجبه من اللَّه حجاب، و لم يحجزه من اللَّه حاجز، و لم يزل ينظر الى اللَّه و ينظر اللَّه اليه حتى يفرغ من حساب الناس:

"Whoever recites these two Surahs: Surah Al-Infitar and Surah Al-Inshiqaq, and keeps them before his eyes in his obligatory and supererogatory prayers, no veil shall veil him from Allah, and no barrier shall stand between him and Allah. He will continuously look toward Allah (with the eye of his heart) and Allah will look upon him (with His grace) until the reckoning of the people is finished." [^1] Certainly, the great blessing of being in the presence of Allah and the removal of veils between him and his Lord is for the one who embeds these two Surahs into the depths of his soul and builds himself based upon them, not merely sufficing with the movement of the tongue.

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 447; and Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 520 (from the book Thawab al-A'mal).

بِسْمِ اللَّهِ الرَّحْمنِ الرَّحِيمِ‌

إِذَا السَّماءُ انْفَطَرَتْ (1) وَ إِذَا الْكَواكِبُ انْتَثَرَتْ (2) وَ إِذَا الْبِحارُ فُجِّرَتْ (3) وَ إِذَا الْقُبُورُ بُعْثِرَتْ (4)

عَلِمَتْ نَفْسٌ ما قَدَّمَتْ وَ أَخَّرَتْ (5)

Commentary

When the Order of the Universe Collapses!

Once again, at the beginning of this Surah, we encounter a portion of the terrifying events that will encompass the entire world on the threshold of the Resurrection.

First, He says:

"When the heaven (the celestial bodies) is cleft asunder" (إِذَا السَّماءُ انْفَطَرَتْ).

"And when the stars are scattered and fall" (وَ إِذَا الْكَواكِبُ انْتَثَرَتْ). The order of the upper world collapses, and massive explosions encompass all celestial bodies. Solar systems lose their order, stars move out of their orbits, and due to violent collisions with one another, they are pulverized. The life of this world comes to an end, and everything is destroyed so that upon its ruins, the new world of the Hereafter may be established.

"Infatarat" is from the root "infitar," meaning to cleave or be split asunder. Similar expressions appear in many verses of the Quran, including: in verse 1 of Surah Al-Inshiqaq, we read إِذَا السَّماءُ انْشَقَّتْ, and in verse 18 of Surah Al-Muzzammil, He says السَّماءُ مُنْفَطِرٌ بِهِ. "Intatharat" is originally from the root "nathr" (on the pattern of nasr), meaning to scatter, and "intithar" means to be scattered. Since the scattering of stars (like a necklace whose string has snapped) causes each to fall in a different corner, many commentators have interpreted it as the falling of stars, which is a necessary consequence of being scattered.

"Kawakib" is the plural of kawkab, which in the Arabic language has many meanings, including: a star in general, the planet Venus specifically, the white spot that appears in the eye, tall plants, tree blossoms, the brilliance of steel, a handsome youth, a sword, water, the leader of a group, and others.

However, it seems its literal meaning is a shining and radiant star, and the rest are considered metaphorical meanings used due to their similarity to the original meaning.

As for what factors cause the scattering of stars, the explosion of celestial bodies, and the disruption of their order—whether the balance of gravity and repulsion is disturbed, or a massive mysterious force overshadows them, or the gradual expansion of the universe (which is proven by scientists today) reaches a point that leads to this result—no one knows for certain. But we know enough to understand that the purpose is to announce that when these massive celestial bodies face such a fate, the situation of weak man is clear! All of this is a warning to man regarding the transience of this world, so that he does not consider it his eternal home, does not become attached to it, and does not stain himself with thousands of sins for its sake.

Then He moves from the heaven to the earth and says: "And when the seas are made to gush forth (and join together)" (وَ إِذَا الْبِحارُ فُجِّرَتْ). Although today all the seas on earth (except for lakes) are connected, it seems that on the threshold of the Resurrection, due to severe earthquakes or the disintegration of mountains and their falling into the seas, the seas will become so full that water will cover all the land, and the seas will become one vast, all-encompassing ocean. This is one of the interpretations of verse 6 of Surah Al-Takwir (وَ إِذَا الْبِحارُ سُجِّرَتْ), as we previously mentioned.

Another possibility given in the interpretation of this verse, as well as verse 6 of Surah Al-Takwir, is that "fujjirat" and "sujjirat" refer to explosions and ignition that turn the oceans into a single mass of fire. As we have noted, water is composed of two elements that are both highly flammable; if factors cause the seawater to decompose into "oxygen" and "hydrogen," a single spark would turn them all into fire!

Then, regarding the second stage of the Resurrection—the stage of the world's rejuvenation and the revival of the dead—He says: "And when the graves are overturned" (وَ إِذَا الْقُبُورُ بُعْثِرَتْ). And the dead come out and are prepared for the reckoning.

"Bu'thirat" means to be overturned and scattered. Raghib in Al-Mufradat does not consider it unlikely that this word is a compound of two words, "ba'th" and "athirat," and thus combines the meaning of both, resulting in the meaning of being overturned and scattered (similar to "basmalah" which is taken from "bism" and "Allah").

In any case, what is mentioned in the above verse is similar to what we read in Surah Al-Zalzalah: وَ أَخَّرَتِ الْأَرْضُ أَثْقالَها: "And the earth throws out its burdens" (assuming it refers to the dead buried in the earth, which is one of the famous interpretations of this verse), or similar to what appears in Surah Al-Nazi'at, verses 13 and 14:

فَإِنَّما هِيَ زَجْرَةٌ واحِدَةٌ فَإِذا هُمْ بِالسَّاهِرَةِ: "It will be but a single shout, and behold, they will be upon the surface of the earth."

All these expressions indicate that the revival and the coming out of the dead from the graves will occur rapidly and suddenly.

After mentioning these signs that occur before and after the Resurrection, He states the final word: "On that day, every soul shall know what it has sent forward and what it has left behind" (عَلِمَتْ نَفْسٌ ما قَدَّمَتْ وَ أَخَّرَتْ). Yes, on that day the veils will be removed, the curtains of arrogance and negligence will be torn, and the realities of the world will become naked and manifest. Since that day is the "Day of Manifestation," everything becomes apparent and visible. It is there that man sees all his deeds and becomes aware of their good and evil—both the deeds he sent forward and the works whose effects remained after him in the world and whose results reached him, such as ongoing charities (sadaqah jariyah), buildings and monuments built for divine or satanic purposes and left behind, or books and scientific or non-scientific works written for good or evil purposes that were utilized by others after him. Likewise, the good or bad traditions (sunan) that led groups of people after him.

These are examples of works whose results reach man after him and are the manifestation of "akhkharat" (left behind) in the above verse.

It is true that man in this world is also generally aware of his deeds, but forgetfulness, selfishness, and self-love often prevent him from remembering them all and realizing the depth of the effects of his actions. However, on that day when a revolution and transformation occur in everything, the soul and spirit of man also undergo such a revolution. It is then that he gains detailed and precise knowledge of all his deeds; rather, according to verse 30 of Surah Al-Imran: يَوْمَ تَجِدُ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ ما عَمِلَتْ مِنْ خَيْرٍ مُحْضَراً وَ ما عَمِلَتْ مِنْ سُوءٍ "The day every soul will find what it has done of good present [before it] and what it has done of evil."

Some commentators have mentioned other interpretations for the verse, including that it refers to deeds prioritized at the beginning of life and deeds postponed to the end of life, but the first interpretation is more appropriate in every respect.

Furthermore, "nafs" (soul) here refers to every human soul and includes all individuals of mankind.

Key Point: The Effects that Man Leaves Behind

In addition to what is mentioned in the above verses, it is clearly understood from Islamic narrations that it is possible for effects to remain from a person whose blessings or evil consequences reach him for many years, even until the Resurrection.

As we read in a hadith from Imam al-Sadiq عليه السلام:

ليس يتبع الرجل بعد موته من الاجر الا ثلاث خصال: صدقة اجراها فى حياته، فهى تجرى بعد موته، و سنة هدى سنها، فهى تعمل بها بعد موته، و ولد صالح يستغفر له:

"After a person's death, his record of deeds is closed, and no reward reaches him except through three ways: charities and useful things he established during his life which continue after his death, a tradition of guidance he initiated which is acted upon after his death, and a righteous child who seeks forgiveness for him." [^1] In another narration, these matters are counted as six things that benefit the believer after death: a righteous child, a Quran he used to recite, a well he dug, a tree he planted, providing water, and a good tradition that remains after him and is followed. ^2 In some narrations, emphasis is placed on the knowledge and science that remains from a person among the people. [^3] Especially regarding good and bad traditions, we have numerous narrations warning people to be careful about what kind of tradition remains among the people as a result of their actions.

The late Tabarsi, in the commentary of the verses under discussion, narrates a hadith to the effect that one day a person stood up in the presence of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله and asked the people for help. Everyone remained silent. At this moment, one of the companions gave something to the petitioner, and the rest followed him and helped the poor man. The Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said:

من استن خيرا فاستن به فله اجره، و مثل اجور من اتبعه، غير منتقص من اجورهم، و من استن شرا فاستن به فعليه وزره، و مثل اوزار من اتبعه غير منتقص من اوزارهم:

"Whoever establishes a good tradition and others follow him, he shall have his reward and the like of the rewards of those who followed him, without anything being diminished from their rewards. And whoever establishes an evil tradition and it is followed, he shall bear his sin and the like of the sins of those who followed him, without anything being diminished from their sins." It was here that Hudhayfah, one of the companions of the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, recited the verse: عَلِمَتْ نَفْسٌ ما قَدَّمَتْ وَ أَخَّرَتْ. [^1] The Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, also says:

فكيف بكم لو تناهت بكم الامور و بعثرت القبورهُنالِكَ تَبْلُوا كُلُّ نَفْسٍ ما أَسْلَفَتْ وَ رُدُّوا إِلَى اللَّهِ مَوْلاهُمُ الْحَقِّ وَ ضَلَّ عَنْهُمْ ما كانُوا يَفْتَرُونَ‌

"How will you be when all matters reach their end, the graves are overturned, and all of you are present in the arena of gathering? There, every soul shall experience what it has previously done, and all shall be returned to Allah, their true Master and Guardian, and that which they used to forge (as partners to Him) shall vanish from them." ^2 These verses and narrations reflect the dimensions of human responsibility for one's actions from the perspective of Islam and show to what extent every human is responsible for his deeds, to the point that even after thousands of years, their effects may create a good reward for him or bring about burden, curse, and sin.

In this regard, we have also discussed in Volume 11, pages 206 to 208 (under verse 25 of Surah Al-Nahl).

[^1]: Bihar al-Anwar, Vol. 71, p. 257.

[^3]: Munyat al-Murid, p. 11.

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 449.

^2: Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 226.

Ayahs

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلْإِنسَـٰنُ مَا غَرَّكَ بِرَبِّكَ ٱلْكَرِيمِ (٦)
O man! What has deceived you about your generous Lord, (6)
ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَكَ فَسَوَّىٰكَ فَعَدَلَكَ (٧)
who created you and proportioned you, and gave you an upright nature, (7)
فِىٓ أَىِّ صُورَةٍ مَّا شَآءَ رَكَّبَكَ (٨)
and composed you in any form that He wished? (8)
كَلَّا بَلْ تُكَذِّبُونَ بِٱلدِّينِ (٩)
No! Indeed, you deny the Retribution. (9)
وَإِنَّ عَلَيْكُمْ لَحَـٰفِظِينَ (١٠)
Indeed, there are over you watchers, (10)
كِرَامًا كَـٰتِبِينَ (١١)
noble writers, (11)
يَعْلَمُونَ مَا تَفْعَلُونَ (١٢)
who know whatever you do. (12)

Commentary

O Human, What Has Deluded You?!

Following the previous verses which spoke about the Resurrection, the verses under discussion first address man with a severe yet affectionate and kind reproachful interrogation to awaken him from the sleep of negligence and draw his attention to his responsibilities before Allah, saying: "O human! What has deluded you concerning your Gracious Lord?" (يَا أَيُّهَا الْإِنْسانُ ما غَرَّكَ بِرَبِّكَ الْكَرِيمِ).

Here, "human" is addressed by his very humanity, reminding him of all his distinctions over other creatures in this world. Then, he is placed before a God who is both "Lord" (Rabb) and "Gracious" (Karim). By virtue of His Lordship, He has constantly kept him under His protection and undertaken his upbringing and perfection; and by virtue of His Grace, He has seated him at the table of His bounties, granting him all material and spiritual blessings without expecting anything from him or seeking reward or compensation. He even overlooks his mistakes and pardons him through His Grace.

Is it fitting for such a noble creature to be audacious toward such a Great Lord? Or to be negligent of Him for even a moment and fall short in fulfilling His commands, which are the guarantee of his own happiness?

Hence, it is narrated in a Hadith from the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله that upon reciting this verse, he said: "His ignorance deluded him." [^1] From this, it becomes clear that the goal is to break man's pride and negligence by relying on the issue of Allah's Lordship and Grace—not as some have imagined, that the goal is to suggest an excuse to man so that he might answer: "Your Grace deluded me."

Furthermore, what is narrated from "Fudayl ibn Iyad"—that he was asked: "If on the Day of Judgment Allah summons you and asks: 'What deluded you concerning your Gracious Lord?', what will you say?" He replied: "I will say: 'The veils You cast over my sins made me negligent and deluded'"—is also not consistent with the meaning of the verse. Rather, it stands in opposition to the primary goal of the verse, because the objective is to shatter pride and awaken one from the sleep of negligence, not to cast a new veil over the veils of negligence! Therefore, it is not appropriate to divert the verse from its intended purpose and draw a conclusion in the opposite direction.

"Deluded you" (gharrak) is from the root ghurur, which originally means negligence while awake; in other words, negligence in a situation where a person should not be negligent. Since negligence sometimes becomes the source of "audacity" or "self-superiority," the word ghurur has also been interpreted with these meanings. Satan is called Gharur (on the weight of sharur) because he deceives man with his whispers and makes him negligent and deluded.

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 449. The same Hadith is narrated in Ad-Durr al-Manthur, Ruh al-Bayan, Ruh al-Ma'ani, and Qurtubi under the verse in question.

Various expressions have been used in the commentary of "Gracious" (Karim). Some have said: The Gracious is a giver whose every act is kindness, and who never seeks to gain profit or repel harm through His giving.

Some have said: The Gracious is one who gives both what is expected of him and what is not required.

And some have said: The Gracious is one who accepts a small commodity and gives a great price in return.

In reality, all of these are gathered in the concept of "Gracious" when considered at the highest level, such as the Grace of Allah. It is enough regarding Allah's Grace that He is not satisfied merely with pardoning the sinner, but rather transforms sins (for those who are worthy) into good deeds.

The Commander of the Faithful, Ali عليه السلام, has extraordinary sentences in the commentary of this verse. He says:

"This verse is the most crushing proof against the listener, and the most definitive excuse against the deluded person whose ignorance has made him audacious and proud. Allah says:

'O human! What has emboldened you to commit your sin? And what has deluded you concerning your Lord? And what has made you enamored with your own destruction?

Is there no cure for this ailment of yours? Or will this sleep of yours not lead to awakening?

Why do you not (at least) have mercy on yourself as much as you have mercy on others? Whenever you see someone in the scorching sun, you shade him; whenever you see a sick person whom pain has severely weakened, you weep for him out of pity. So what has made you so patient with your own illness, so enduring of your own calamity, and what has consoled you from weeping for yourself? While you are the most beloved of people to yourself! How has the fear of the descent of calamity at night not kept you awake, while you are drowning in sin and disobedience to Him, and while you are under His dominion?'

Tafsir Nemouneh, Vol. 26, p. 220

'Treat this disease (negligence of God) with the medicine of determination and firm resolve, and dispel this sleep of negligence that has covered your eyes with wakefulness. Come, be obedient to Allah, and find intimacy in His remembrance. Imagine well that during your negligence of God, He cares for you by granting blessings, calls you toward His pardon and forgiveness, and places you under the cover of His favor and blessings.

While you continue to turn your back on Him and turn toward another. Great is the Lord who, with this mighty power, is "Gracious," yet you, with this weakness and insignificance, how audacious you are in disobeying Him!'" ... [^1]

Then, to awaken this negligent human, He points to a corner of His Grace and favors toward him in four stages, saying: "The One who created you, then fashioned you, then balanced you." (الَّذِي خَلَقَكَ فَسَوَّاكَ فَعَدَلَكَ). "In whatever form He willed, He assembled you." (فِي أَيِّ صُورَةٍ ما شاءَ رَكَّبَكَ) [^2]

[^1]: Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 223.

[^2]: "Ma" in "Ma sha'" is extra (for emphasis), and some have suggested it might be conditional, but the first possibility is more appropriate.

In this way, He describes the four great stages of creation—namely, the origin of creation, then organization, then balancing, and finally assembly—in short and profound phrases.

In the first stage is the origin of human creation, where He created him from an insignificant drop of fluid in the darkness of the womb.

In the next stage, which is the stage of "fashioning and organization" (taswiyah), He proportioned every limb of his body with an amazing order. The eyes, ears, heart, vessels, and other organs are such that if a person reflects on the structure and system of any one of them alone, and observes the divine favors and gifts in each, he will see a world of knowledge, power, kindness, and grace manifested before his eyes.

These are favors that natural scientists have been reflecting upon, speaking about, and writing about for thousands of years, and it is still only the beginning of the book; they remain at the very start of describing Him.

Then comes the turn for another third favor, which is "balancing between the powers and limbs of the body and their harmony and relationship with one another" (ta'dil).

The human body is mainly created of two parts that are counterparts to each other: hands and feet, eyes and ears, skeletal structures, vessels, nerves, and muscles in the two parts of the body are harmonious and complementary to each other.

Furthermore, different organs complete each other's work. For example, the respiratory system helps the blood circulation, and the circulatory system helps respiration. To swallow a morsel of food, the teeth, tongue, salivary glands, muscles around the mouth, and the throat all join hands so that a morsel enters the digestive system. From then on, many harmonies take place so that the food is digested and absorbed by the body, creating the necessary energy for effort and movement. All of these are gathered in the phrase "then balanced you" (fa'adalak).

Some have considered this phrase an allusion to man being upright in stature among all types of animals, which is considered a virtue and advantage for him. This meaning is more compatible with the next stage, but the previous meaning is more comprehensive.

Finally, the stage of "assembly" and "shaping" him in comparison with other creatures arrives. Yes, Allah has given the human species a balanced, beautiful, and original form compared to types of animals and beasts. Likewise, a beautiful inner character coupled with an awakened primordial nature (fitrah), and a composition ready to accept any kind of knowledge, awareness, and education.

Beyond this, the faces of individual humans differ from one another, and as stated in verse 22 of Surah Ar-Rum: "The diversity of your languages and colors are among the signs of Allah" (وَ مِنْ آياتِهِ خَلْقُ السَّماواتِ وَ الْأَرْضِ وَ اخْتِلافُ أَلْسِنَتِكُمْ وَ أَلْوانِكُمْ). If this difference did not exist, the system of human social life would be disrupted.

In addition to this outward difference, He has placed differences in talents, tastes, and temperaments, and assembled and organized them into forms that His wisdom required, so that from their totality, a healthy society emerges that provides for all its needs, and the outward and inward powers of humans complement each other.

In total, as stated in verse 4 of Surah At-Tin: "We have certainly created man in the best of statures" (لَقَدْ خَلَقْنَا الْإِنْسانَ فِي أَحْسَنِ تَقْوِيمٍ). Allah has created this human in the best form.

In short, in these verses and many other verses of the Quran, Allah compels this forgetful and deluded human to attain self-knowledge—from the beginning of creation in the mother's womb until the moment of birth, and from then until full growth and development—to examine his own existence closely and see that at every step and every moment, a new blessing from that Great Benefactor has come to him, so that he may see himself entirely drowned in His kindness, descend from the mount of pride and negligence, and place the collar of servitude to the Truth upon his neck.

Then the Quran goes to the source of their pride and negligence, saying: It is not as you imagine, "But no! You deny the Recompense." (كَلَّا بَلْ تُكَذِّبُونَ بِالدِّينِ).

Neither is the issue of Allah's Grace the cause of your pride, nor His kindness and blessings; rather, the main root must be found in the lack of faith in the Day of Resurrection. [^1] Yes, when we look closely at the state of deluded and negligent individuals, we see that the main issue is that doubt, hesitation, or denial of the Resurrection is hidden in the depths of the souls of many of them, and other matters are all excuses. For this reason, if the foundations of faith in the Hereafter are strengthened in hearts, this pride and negligence will be removed.

The meaning of "Din" here is the Recompense and the Day of Recompense. The possibility suggested by some that it means "the religion of Islam" seems unlikely, as the axis of discussion in these verses is the Resurrection.

Then, to eliminate the factors of pride and negligence and to strengthen faith in the Resurrection, it adds: "And indeed, over you are surely keepers," (وَ إِنَّ عَلَيْكُمْ لَحافِظِينَ) [^1]

"Noble recorders," (كِراماً كاتِبِينَ) who are near and honored by the Lord and constantly write your deeds.

"They know whatever you do," (يَعْلَمُونَ ما تَفْعَلُونَ) and they know it all well.

The meaning of "keepers" (hafizin) here are the angels who are commissioned to guard and preserve the deeds of humans, whether good or bad. In verse 17 of Surah Qaf, they are referred to as "the observer and the ready" (raqib and atid): "Man does not utter any word except that with him is an observer ready [to record]." And in the same Surah Qaf, in the preceding verse, it says: "When the two receivers receive, seated on the right and on the left."

[^1]: "Kalla" is a word of deterrence and for denying something previously mentioned or imagined. Commentators have given many possibilities as to what "Kalla" denies in this verse, the best of which is what was mentioned above: that "Kalla" here denies all sources of pride and negligence except the denial of the Resurrection mentioned after "bal." Raghib in Al-Mufradat (under the root bal) chose this meaning.

In other verses of the Quran, the existence of these angels monitoring deeds is also mentioned.

Undoubtedly, Allah is the witness and observer of man's deeds before anyone else and better than anyone else, but for further emphasis and a greater sense of responsibility, He has appointed many monitors, one group of whom are these very angels.

In addition to these, other monitors surround man from every side, the totality of whom we have mentioned under the title "Witnesses in the Court of Resurrection" in seven parts (under verses 20 and 21 of Surah Fussilat), and they are briefly listed here:

First, the Pure Essence of Allah, who says: "Whatever deed you do, We are Witness and Observer over you" (Yunus: 61).

Then the Prophets and the Successors (An-Nisa: 41).

After that, the tongue, hands, feet, and generally the limbs of the human body (An-Nur: 24).

And the skin of the human body (Fussilat: 21).

And the angels (Qaf: 21) and the verse under discussion.

And then the earth upon which man lives and where obedience and sin occur (Az-Zalzalah: 4).

And finally, the time in which the deeds are performed (Safinat al-Bihar, Vol. 2, root yawm).

In Ihtijaj al-Tabarsi, it is narrated that someone asked Imam as-Sadiq عليه السلام: "What is the reason for the existence of angels commissioned to record the good and bad deeds of humans, while we know that Allah is the Knower of the secret and what is even more hidden?" The Imam عليه السلام replied:

"He made them worship Him through that, and made them witnesses over His creation, so that the servants, due to their constant presence, would be more diligent in obeying Allah and more restrained from disobeying Him. How many a servant intends to commit a sin, then remembers their place and desists and refrains, saying: 'My Lord sees me, and my keepers testify to this against me.' Furthermore, Allah, by His compassion and kindness, has put them in charge of His servants to repel the rebellious devils and the vermin of the earth and many harms that they do not see, by Allah's permission, until the command of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic, arrives." [^1]

From this narration, it is well understood that in addition to the mission of recording deeds, they are commissioned to protect man from unfortunate accidents, harms, and the whispers of devils.

(A detailed explanation regarding the various duties of divine angels and their missions is provided in Vol. 18, pp. 173-177, under verse 1 of Surah Fatir).

It is noteworthy that the above verses, in describing these angels, say: "They are noble and of high status before the Lord," so that humans may be more careful of their deeds; for the higher the status of the observer of one's deeds, the more one respects them and the more ashamed one becomes of committing a sin.

The expression "recorders" (katibin) is in fact an emphasis on the meaning that they do not rely solely on memory, but rather write and record precisely. In this way, nothing ever escapes them, and they record both the small and the great.

The expression "They know whatever you do" is again a renewed emphasis on the fact that they are aware of all your deeds without exception, and their writing is based on this awareness.

Incidentally, all these expressions indicate the choice and free will of man, for if man had no choice of his own, appointing all these monitors to record deeds and all these warnings and notices would have no correct purpose or meaning.

On the other hand, all of this indicates the reality that the issue of divine reckoning and recompense is very serious, as Allah has given it extraordinary importance. Awareness and faith in this reality are sufficient to educate a person, acquaint him with his responsibilities, and have an extraordinary deterrent effect on wrongdoings.

Key Point: The Recorders of Deeds

Not only in the above verses but in many other verses of the Quran and Islamic narrations, it is mentioned that Allah has appointed monitors for every human who write his deeds, both good and bad, and prepare the record of deeds for the Day of Recompense.

Regarding the characteristics of these angels, profound and warning expressions have appeared in Islamic narrations, including:

  1. Someone asked Imam Musa ibn Ja'far عليه السلام: "Do the two angels commissioned to record man's deeds also become aware of his inner intention and resolve at the time of sin or a good deed?"

The Imam عليه السلام replied: "Is the smell of a cesspool and perfume the same?" The narrator said: "No!" The Imam عليه السلام said: "When a person intends a good deed, his breath becomes fragrant! The angel on the right (who is commissioned to record good deeds) says to the angel on the left: 'Rise, for he has intended a good deed.' And when he performs it, that person's tongue becomes the pen of that angel, and his saliva becomes his ink, and he records it. But when he intends a sin, his breath becomes foul-smelling! The angel on the left says to the angel on the right: 'Rise, for he has intended a sin.' And when he performs it, his tongue is the pen of that angel and his saliva is his ink, and he writes it!" [^1]

This Hadith clearly shows that man's intention affects his entire being, and the angels become aware of his inner secrets through outward signs. Certainly, if they were not aware, they could not record man's deeds properly, because the nature of the intention is extremely effective in the value, sincerity, or pollution of the deed, to the extent that the Noble Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله said in a famous Hadith: "Actions are but by intentions."

Furthermore, it becomes clear that they take from the person himself and spend it for him; our tongue is their pen, and our saliva is their ink!

  1. They are commissioned to record it as one "good deed" when a person intends a good deed, and when he performs it, they write ten good deeds. But when he resolves to commit a sin, they write nothing against him until he acts, and after the act, they record only one sin. [^1] This expression indicates the ultimate Grace and Favor of Allah upon man, that He forgives the intention of sin and recompenses the act of sin according to the scales of justice, but in the path of obedience, every intention is a good deed, and He rewards every good act according to Favor, not according to the scale of justice. This is an encouragement for performing good deeds.

  2. In another narration, it is stated that the Messenger of Allah صلى الله عليه وآله, after pointing to the existence of these two angels and the writing of good deeds with a tenfold reward, said: "When a person performs a bad deed, the right angel says to the left angel: 'Do not hurry in writing this sin; perhaps he will perform a good deed after it that will cover his sin, just as Allah the Great says: "Indeed, good deeds do away with misdeeds," or perhaps he will repent and seek forgiveness (and the effect of the sin will vanish)...' and he refrains from writing the bad deed for seven hours. If no good deed or seeking of forgiveness follows it, the angel of good deeds says to the angel of bad deeds: 'Write it against this deprived wretch!'" [^2]

  3. In another Hadith from Imam as-Sadiq عليه السلام, it is said: "When believers speak together in a private gathering, the keepers of deeds say to one another: 'Let us move away from them; perhaps they have a secret that Allah has kept hidden!'" [^1]

  4. Ali عليه السلام, in one of his sermons in which he invited people to piety, says: "Know, O servants of Allah, that monitors from yourselves have been appointed over you, and observers from your own limbs. And know that truthful reckoners record your deeds, and even keep the count of your breaths. Neither the darkness of a dark night hides you from them, nor do firm and closed doors. And how close is tomorrow to today!" [^2]

[^1]: Usul al-Kafi, Vol. 2, Chapter on "One who intends a good or bad deed," Hadith 3. [^1]: Same source, Hadith 1 and 2. [^2]: Usul al-Kafi, Vol. 2, Chapter on "One who intends a good or bad deed," Hadith 4. [^1]: Usul al-Kafi, as quoted in Nur al-Thaqalayn, Vol. 5, p. 110. [^2]: Nahj al-Balagha, Sermon 157.

Ayahs

إِنَّ ٱلْأَبْرَارَ لَفِى نَعِيمٍ (١٣)
Indeed the pious shall be amid bliss, (13)
وَإِنَّ ٱلْفُجَّارَ لَفِى جَحِيمٍ (١٤)
and indeed the vicious shall be in hell, (14)
يَصْلَوْنَهَا يَوْمَ ٱلدِّينِ (١٥)
entering it on the Day of Retribution, (15)
وَمَا هُمْ عَنْهَا بِغَآئِبِينَ (١٦)
and they shall not be absent from it. (16)
وَمَآ أَدْرَىٰكَ مَا يَوْمُ ٱلدِّينِ (١٧)
And what will show you what is the Day of Retribution? (17)
ثُمَّ مَآ أَدْرَىٰكَ مَا يَوْمُ ٱلدِّينِ (١٨)
Again, what will show you what is the Day of Retribution? (18)
يَوْمَ لَا تَمْلِكُ نَفْسٌ لِّنَفْسٍ شَيْـًٔا ۖ وَٱلْأَمْرُ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِّلَّهِ (١٩)
It is a day when no soul will be of any avail to another soul and all command that day will belong to Allah. (19)

Commentary

The Day When No Soul Shall Avail Another!

Following the discussion in the previous verses regarding the recording of human deeds by the angels, these verses point to the result of this auditing and the final destination of the righteous and the wicked, stating: "Indeed, the righteous shall be in [a state of] great Divine bliss" (إِنَّ الْأَبْرارَ لَفِي نَعِيمٍ).

"And indeed, the wicked shall be in Hell!" (وَ إِنَّ الْفُجَّارَ لَفِي جَحِيمٍ). "Abrār" is the plural of "Bārr" and "Barr" (rhyming with Haqq), meaning a righteous person, and "Birr" (with a kasra on the Bā) refers to every type of goodness. Here, it encompasses "righteous beliefs," "good intentions," and "virtuous deeds." "Na‘īm" is a singular noun meaning blessing or bounty, and here it refers to the eternal Paradise. Its mention in the "indefinite" form (nakirah) is to express the importance, vastness, and greatness of this blessing, the extent of which no one truly knows except Allah. The choice of "Na‘īm," which is a Sifah Mushabbahah (adjective resembling a participle), serves as an emphasis on the permanence and continuity of this blessing, as this concept is usually inherent in this grammatical form.

"Fujjār" is the plural of "Fājir," originally derived from "Fajr," meaning a wide rupture or tearing. The break of dawn is called "Fajr" because it is as if the black curtain of night is completely torn asunder by the morning light. Consequently, the word "Fujūr" is used for the actions of those who tear the veil of modesty and piety and step into the path of sin.

"Jahīm" is derived from the root "Jaham" (rhyming with Fahm), meaning to kindle a fire. Therefore, "Jahīm" refers to a blazing and flaming fire, and in Quranic terminology, it usually refers to Hell.

The statement "the righteous are in Paradise, and the wicked are in Hell" may mean that they have already entered Paradise and Hell even now, and that the blessings of Paradise and the torments of Hell encompass them in this very world, just as we read in Surah Al-Ankabut, verse 54: "And indeed, Hell is encompassing of the disbelievers" (وَ إِنَّ جَهَنَّمَ لَمُحِيطَةٌ بِالْكافِرِينَ).

However, some have said that such expressions refer to a certain future, for in Arabic literature, a certain future and a "realized present tense" are expressed in the present or sometimes the past tense (the first meaning is more consistent with the appearance of the verse, but the second meaning is more appropriate).

In the next verse, providing more explanation regarding the fate of the wicked, it adds: "They will enter it on the Day of Recompense [and burn in its fire]" (يَصْلَوْنَها يَوْمَ الدِّينِ).

If the meaning of the previous verse is that they are already inside Hell, this verse shows that on the Day of Resurrection, they will experience a deeper and more intense entry into this blazing fire and will fully feel its effects.

"Yaslawn" is derived from the root "Salā" (rhyming with Sa‘y), meaning to enter the fire, to burn, to be roasted, and to endure its pain and suffering. Since it is in the present/future tense (Mudāri‘), it indicates continuity and permanence.

And again, for further emphasis, He says: "And they will never be absent from it" (وَ ما هُمْ عَنْها بِغائِبِينَ). Many commentators have taken this sentence as evidence for the eternity and everlasting nature of the punishment for the "Fujjār." They have concluded that "Fujjār" in these verses refers to the disbelievers, as "eternity" (khulūd) does not exist except for them. Therefore, the "Fujjār" are those who have torn the veils of piety and modesty due to a lack of faith and denial of the Day of Recompense, not merely due to the temporary triumph of carnal desires while still possessing faith.

The expression of this verse in the "present tense" is again an emphasis on what was previously mentioned: that such individuals are not entirely absent or far from Hell even in this world. Their very life is a hell, and their grave, according to the famous Hadith, is a pit from the pits of fire. Thus, the hell of this world, the hell of Barzakh, and the hell of Resurrection are all prepared for them.

Furthermore, the above verse conveys the reality that the punishment of the people of Hell has no intermission, and they are not distanced from it even for an hour or a moment.

Then, to express the significance of that Great Day, He adds: "And what will make you realize what the Day of Recompense is?" (وَ ما أَدْراكَ ما يَوْمُ الدِّينِ).

"Again, what will make you realize what the Day of Recompense is?" (ثُمَّ ما أَدْراكَ ما يَوْمُ الدِّينِ).

When the Prophet صلى الله عليه وآله, with his vast knowledge of the Resurrection and his extraordinary insight into the Beginning and the End, cannot fully [be described as having] grasped the events of that Great Day and the immense anxiety and horror that govern it, the situation for others is clear.

This statement reflects the reality that the dimensions of the terrifying events of the Resurrection are so vast and great that they cannot be described by any words. Just as we, the prisoners of this earthly world, are not truly aware of the endless blessings of Paradise, we cannot be fully aware of the punishment of Hell and the events of that Great Day in general.

Then, in a short and meaningful sentence, He presents another characteristic of that Day, in which everything is essentially hidden, saying: "The Day when no soul shall have power to do anything for another soul, and the command, that Day, is [entirely] with Allah" (يَوْمَ لا تَمْلِكُ نَفْسٌ لِنَفْسٍ شَيْئاً وَ الْأَمْرُ يَوْمَئِذٍ لِلَّهِ). Of course, in this world too, all affairs are in the hand of His power, and everyone is in need of Him. However, here there are at least apparent owners, rulers, and commanders whom superficial and unaware individuals sometimes perceive as independent sources of power. But on that Day, even this apparent and metaphorical ownership and sovereignty will be folded away, and the absolute sovereignty of Allah and His ownership over everything will be more manifest than ever.

This is the same as what has appeared in other verses of the Quran: "To whom belongs the sovereignty this Day? To Allah, the One, the Irresistible" (Ghafir: 16).

Fundamentally, on that Day, everyone is so preoccupied with themselves that even if they supposedly had power, they would not attend to another: "For every man, that Day, will be a matter to occupy him" (Abasa: 37).

In a Hadith from Imam al-Baqir عليه السلام, we read: "The command on that Day and every day is for Allah... when the Day of Resurrection comes, all rulers will perish, and no ruler will remain except Allah." [1]

Here a question arises: Does this expression contradict the issue of the "Intercession" (Shafā‘at) of the Prophets, the Awliyā’, and the angels?

The answer to this question becomes clear from the discussions we have previously had regarding intercession: the Quran explicitly states in numerous verses that intercession is also by the permission and leave of Allah, and the intercessors of the Day of Recompense do not intercede for anyone except by His pleasure: "And they do not intercede except for him whom He approves" (Al-Anbiya: 28).

O Allah! On that terrifying Day, all eyes are fixed upon You; we too, today,

[1]: Majma‘ al-Bayān, Vol. 10, p. 450.

also have our eyes turned toward You.

O Lord! Do not deprive us of Your endless favors in this world and the next.

O God! In every state, the absolute Ruler is You; protect us from falling into the valley of Shirk and seeking refuge in others.

(Amin, O Lord of the Worlds) End of Surah al-Infitār
